stories.headline: Kidnappers of Oyo schoolchildren and teachers demand N1bn ransom and Sharia law as rescue stalls into fourth week
stories.summary: Gunmen who seized dozens of pupils and teachers from three schools in Oriire, Oyo State on May 15 have issued fresh demands — a 1 billion naira ransom, the release of detained associates, and the imposition of Sharia law in the state — with roughly 32 captives still held. The Oyo government says it will not pay ransom, teachers launched an indefinite strike, and Nigeria's human-rights commission demanded the captives' urgent release.

perspectives.keyFraming: US wires treat this as 'banditry'; Nigerian coverage centers the kidnappers' explicit political demand to impose Sharia law and free detained commanders.
perspectives.summary: Nigerian outlets report abductors are demanding a 1 billion naira ransom plus the implementation of Sharia law in Oyo and the release of detained associates. Official tallies cite 18 primary pupils, seven secondary students and seven teachers still held, with one teacher killed in captivity. The state rejects negotiation and ransom while difficult terrain and fears for hostage safety hamper any rescue.

sources.title: Nigeria: Abductors Demand Sharia Law, N1bn Ransom for Release of Oyo Pupils, Teachers
Oyo, Borno Abductions: Tough Terrain, Casualty Fears Stall Rescue Mission

stories.headline: Deadly clashes erupt in Mogadishu as opposition rejects Somali president's one-year term extension
stories.summary: Government forces and opposition-aligned militias exchanged heavy gunfire in Mogadishu for a second day around the homes of two former leaders, ahead of a banned June 5 protest. The fighting followed parliament's vote to extend President Hassan Sheikh Mohamud's term by a year after it expired on May 15, which the opposition calls a 'constitutional coup.' The UN and US Embassy condemned the violence.

perspectives.keyFraming: Centers the electoral-integrity dispute and the opposition's 'constitutional coup' argument over the May 15 term expiry — not generic 'Africa instability.'
perspectives.summary: Regional coverage framed the violence as the latest escalation of a constitutional crisis triggered by parliament extending Mohamud's expired mandate, foregrounding the opposition's legal case and the risk to Somalia's fragile politics.

sources.title: Why Mogadishu clashes are deepening Somalia's political crisis again

perspectives.keyFraming: Foregrounds that internationally trained, anti-al-Shabab security forces were turned on domestic political rivals — the angle US wires omit.
perspectives.summary: Wire and analysis coverage reported a second day of heavy gunfire around the residences of ex-PM Khaire and ex-President Sharif Sheikh Ahmed, with the UN and US Embassy condemning the violence ahead of a banned protest.

sources.title: Violence erupts in Somalia's capital over president's extended term
Clashes Erupt in Mogadishu as Somali Forces and Opposition Militias Trade Fire

stories.headline: Brussels education-cuts protest turns violent as police fire tear gas and water cannon
stories.summary: Thousands of students and teachers clashed with police in central Brussels on June 4 over a plan to raise university tuition about 35% and add unpaid teaching hours, part of €300m in education savings. A largely peaceful march escalated near Brussels Central Station, with barricades and scooters set alight and windows smashed; police responded with tear gas and water cannon. Calmer parallel protests hit Namur and Charleroi.

perspectives.keyFraming: Carries the government's deficit justification alongside protester grievances — the both-sides budget context US outlets skip.
perspectives.summary: Aggregated coverage paired the protesters' grievances over tuition and working conditions with the French Community government's argument that €300m in education savings are needed to close a €1.9bn deficit.

sources.title: Brussels unrest: education reform sparks violent protests

stories.headline: Libyans blockade UNHCR's Tripoli office in anti-migrant protests as the UN denies any resettlement plans
stories.summary: On June 4, hundreds of Libyans blocked the UN refugee agency's Tripoli headquarters, demanding undocumented migrants leave and the office close, chanting 'Libya belongs to Libyans.' UNHCR responded June 5 denying it runs any migrant-resettlement program in Libya. Libya hosts roughly 900,000 migrants among about 7 million people.

perspectives.keyFraming: Arab and regional outlets frame this as a domestic anti-migrant backlash in a key Mediterranean transit state — a Europe-bound migration pressure point US desks overlook.
perspectives.summary: Regional coverage reported the blockade of the UNHCR office and the protesters' demands, situating it within Libya's role as a major transit hub for migrants attempting to reach Europe.

sources.title: Hundreds in Libya protest against migrants, refugees

perspectives.keyFraming: State media centers the UN's denial and rumor management, framing the episode as institutional reassurance rather than xenophobic mobilization.
perspectives.summary: Chinese state media reported both the protest shutting the UNHCR office and the UN mission's subsequent denial of any migrant-resettlement program, foregrounding the agency's clarification.

sources.title: UN mission denies allegations of migrant resettlement in Libya

stories.headline: Bolivia nears state of emergency as five-week blockade siege of La Paz triggers resignations and oil-field seizures
stories.summary: A protest movement that began in early May over fuel shortages, high inflation and austerity has hardened into scores of road blockades across seven departments, with at least nine reported dead and roughly $1.6 billion in losses. On June 3 President Rodrigo Paz sent a state-of-emergency bill to the Assembly as two ministers resigned; days later protesters briefly seized an oil field as La Paz hospitals declared an oxygen emergency.

perspectives.keyFraming: Centers the humanitarian toll — blocked ambulances, oxygen-starved hospitals — and indigenous and labor demands rather than the 'attempted coup' framing.
perspectives.summary: Latin American coverage foregrounded the human cost of the blockades, including deaths from blocked ambulances and a medical-oxygen emergency, while detailing the sectoral and indigenous grievances driving five weeks of unrest against the Paz government.

sources.title: Bolivia's blockade crisis reopens a deep racial divide

stories.headline: Peru heads to tight June 7 presidential runoff between Keiko Fujimori and Roberto Sanchez
stories.summary: Peru votes June 7 in a presidential runoff pitting right-wing Keiko Fujimori against leftist Roberto Sanchez, after a first round marred by count delays and logistical problems. Late polling showed the race within a few points. The contest caps years of political instability and could reshape the direction of the Andean nation, but has drawn little US front-page attention.

perspectives.keyFraming: Explains the Fujimori-dynasty-versus-left stakes and the credibility cloud from first-round count failures — context US wires skip for a one-line result.
perspectives.summary: International coverage laid out the runoff matchup and the stakes for a country wracked by years of presidential turnover, noting questions over the first round's delayed and troubled count as Peruvians prepare to choose between Fujimori and Sanchez.

sources.title: Fujimori vs Sanchez: What to know about Peru's presidential run-off election

stories.headline: Mali's junta places roughly €3m bounty on al-Qaeda Sahel leader Iyad Ag Ghali after April mega-offensive
stories.summary: On June 5 Mali's military government offered a 2 billion CFA franc (about €3m/$3.5m) reward for the capture or 'neutralisation' of JNIM chief Iyad Ag Ghali, plus 1.5 billion CFA for his deputy Amadou Koufa. It follows JNIM's April 25 coordinated assault on Bamako, Kati and four other cities that killed Mali's defence minister, the largest jihadist operation since 2012.

perspectives.keyFraming: African outlets read the bounty as an admission the junta cannot win militarily and is now openly pricing the heads of leaders it once negotiated with — an angle US wires skip.
perspectives.summary: African coverage emphasized the bounty signals the junta's difficulty after the April 25 offensive reached Bamako and killed the defence minister. Ag Ghali is a former Malian diplomat already under a US terror designation; the reward scheme also targets Tuareg FLA commanders, underscoring the alliance reshaping the war. US front pages carried no coverage of the announcement.

sources.title: Mali junta puts €3m bounty on Sahel al-Qaeda leader Ag Ghali
Mali junta offers $3.5M reward for jihadist leader
Mali places N5bn equivalent bounty on top terrorist leaders

stories.headline: Africa CDC and WHO launch $518M Ebola response plan as DRC and Uganda death tolls climb
stories.summary: On June 5, Africa CDC and the WHO unveiled a joint continental plan seeking $518 million through November 2026 to fight the Bundibugyo-strain Ebola outbreak declared a public-health emergency on May 17. The DRC has reported hundreds of cases and dozens of deaths, with Uganda also affected; responders cite attacks on burial teams, misinformation and mistrust as major obstacles.

perspectives.keyFraming: Centers African institutions financing and leading their own outbreak response, an angle missed by US coverage focused on protecting Americans.
perspectives.summary: Al Jazeera framed the $518 million plan as Africa CDC and WHO mobilizing a continental, African-led response amid rising tolls in the DRC and Uganda, foregrounding operational obstacles such as attacks on frontline workers and rumours rather than the risk to the West.

sources.title: WHO and Africa CDC unveil $518m Ebola plan as DRC, Uganda death tolls rise

perspectives.keyFraming: Official multilateral framing of funding needs and the six-month response window US wires skipped.
perspectives.summary: WHO's announcement detailed the joint Africa CDC-WHO plan to raise $518 million to help African countries detect and respond to the Bundibugyo outbreak from June to November 2026, emphasizing surveillance, treatment-center capacity and regional preparedness.

sources.title: Africa CDC and WHO launch joint continental Ebola response plan
World News in Brief: UN scales up Ebola response, refugees 'exiled' in Africa, Political tensions escalate in Somalia

stories.headline: South Africa braces for June 30 anti-migrant 'national shutdown' as Ramaphosa rejects xenophobia label
stories.summary: Anti-immigration movements including Operation Dudula have set a June 30 ultimatum for undocumented migrants to leave and threatened a national shutdown that officials fear could echo the 2021 riots. On June 5 the Presidency ruled out any shutdown and warned against vigilantism, and Ramaphosa, speaking during Kenyan President Ruto's state visit, insisted 'South Africans are not xenophobic' while pledging tougher enforcement. Other African governments have begun repatriating nationals.

perspectives.keyFraming: US wires miss that this is a state-vs-vigilante sovereignty crisis: the government is racing to pre-empt a citizen 'shutdown' that could trigger lethal violence and a regional diplomatic rupture across Africa.
perspectives.summary: South African and pan-African outlets frame the June 30 ultimatum as a test of whether Pretoria can contain organized xenophobic vigilantism without conceding to demands for a moratorium on refugee applications. Coverage emphasizes Ramaphosa's defensive posture during Ruto's visit, the economic threat of a shutdown, and other African governments beginning to repatriate citizens.

sources.title: Ramaphosa to address South Africa's migration crisis as Ntshavheni rules out shutdown
South Africa: Ramaphosa Rejects Xenophobia Label, Calls for African Solutions

stories.headline: Iranian student protests over university-entrance (Konkur) GPA rule spread to about 20 provinces
stories.summary: Thousands of Iranian high-school students protested across roughly 20 provinces on June 6 against a rule making high-school GPA a mandatory share of the Konkur entrance-exam score, which they say undermines educational equality. Demonstrations reached Tehran, Mashhad, Shiraz, Isfahan and Yazd; students were injured in Yazd and arrested in Qom and Mashhad.

perspectives.keyFraming: A non-political grievance over exam fairness snowballs into open youth defiance of the state — a domestic-discontent angle US wires skip while focused on Iran's external conflicts.
perspectives.summary: Iran International reports protests widened over final exams and university-entrance rules, with students staging a sit-in outside Tehran's Supreme Council of the Cultural Revolution and chanting against 'empty promises,' vowing not to disperse until their demands are met.

sources.title: Iran student protests widen over final exams and university entrance rules

perspectives.keyFraming: Quantifies the geographic spread and repression US outlets ignore — some 20 provinces, with arrests and violence over an exam rule.
perspectives.summary: The Times of Israel, citing Iran International, reports students protested in some 20 provinces over exam changes begun in late May; protests reached Tehran, Isfahan, West Azarbaijan, Khuzestan, Fars, Qom, Lorestan and Yazd, with injuries in Yazd and an arrest in Qom; video showed students chanting 'Justice, education, our undeniable right.'

sources.title: Iranian students hold protests in some 20 provinces over exam changes; some met with arrests, violence

stories.headline: Three Kuki villagers killed in pre-dawn Manipur attack trigger 35-km coffin rally and district shutdown
stories.summary: Armed assailants attacked Loibol Khullen village in Kangpokpi district of India's Manipur state around 4am on June 5, killing three Kuki-Zo residents and burning seven houses. On June 6 thousands of mourners staged a 35-km coffin rally amid a total district shutdown that blocked the NH-2 highway, demanding justice and central-government action. The killings reignite a Meitei-Kuki ethnic conflict that has run since May 2023.

perspectives.keyFraming: Indian northeastern outlets center the renewed Kuki-Zo grievance and demand New Delhi act — an angle US wires skip entirely.
perspectives.summary: Regional coverage stresses that the victims included a young family, fueling outrage across Kuki-Zo areas, and that the coffin rally carried the message that bloodshed must stop. Reporting situates the attack within the unresolved 2023 ethnic war and ongoing displacement, with Kuki bodies seeking an independent probe and the chief minister condemning the killings.

sources.title: 'Bloodshed Must Stop': Thousands March in Manipur's Kangpokpi as Loibol Victims Laid to Rest
Three civilians killed, houses damaged in Manipur's Kangpokpi village attack
Three Killed, Seven Houses Burnt in Suspected Militant Attack in Manipur's Kangpokpi

stories.headline: JNIM fuel blockade chokes Mali's capital as militants mine highways and junta bans motorcycles
stories.summary: Al-Qaeda affiliate JNIM's fuel blockade on Bamako and southern Mali is deepening into siege warfare. A June 1 landmine on the Bamako-Kayes highway killed eight and injured dozens aboard a bus, and on June 3 the junta banned certain motorcycles favored by jihadist groups. With hundreds of fuel tankers destroyed since the blockade began in September 2025, the landlocked country faces fuel queues, school closures, blackouts and surging food prices.

perspectives.keyFraming: Analysts frame this as a slow-motion economic siege of a capital that US front pages ignore for lacking a single dramatic battle.
perspectives.summary: AP coverage documents the cascading civilian toll: fuel rationed to the military, schools repeatedly shuttered, healthcare cold chains failing and prices spiking, with the June 1 highway mine and June 3 motorcycle ban the latest escalation in JNIM's bid to throttle Bamako and pressure the junta.

sources.title: Mali is squeezed as militants enforce a fuel blockade

stories.headline: Indonesia jails deputy immigration minister in widening corruption sweep touching Prabowo's meals program
stories.summary: Indonesia's Corruption Eradication Commission detained Deputy Immigration Minister Silmy Karim on June 4, naming him and eight others as suspects in a scheme to extort foreign nationals over residency permits, with at least Rp 145.5 billion allegedly collected. The arrest followed the detention of the recently sacked head of the National Nutrition Agency over graft in President Prabowo's flagship free-meals program, marking an unusual high-level anti-corruption push.

perspectives.keyFraming: Regional outlets frame the sweep as a test of Prabowo's anti-graft credibility and a risk to his signature meals program.
perspectives.summary: Indonesian outlets emphasize the scale of the alleged extortion and the symbolic blow of arresting officials tied to Prabowo's nutrition flagship just as it faces cost overruns and food-poisoning cases. The KPK framed this as part of an institutional campaign of stings rather than a one-off action.

sources.title: Deputy immigration minister suspected of extorting foreigners over stay permit
